findings.description: Nested <main> landmark elements - ensure the document has at most one main landmark at top level
findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 37. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: The PDF agenda is formatted as a dotted-leader list (not a table), but the HTML renders it as a formal table with borders, rows, and columns. While the data content is preserved, the structural representation has changed from a simple list/agenda format to a bordered table.
findings.evidence: PDF shows time slots and topics connected by dot leaders in a flowing text format. HTML shows the same data organized in a bordered table with distinct Time, Topic, and Presenter columns.

findings.description: The HTML adds column headers 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter' to the agenda table. These headers do not appear in the source PDF, which presents the agenda as a simple list format without explicit column headers.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <th>Time</th><th>Topic</th><th>Presenter</th> — these labels are not present in the PDF. However, they are reasonable structural inferences from the content layout.

findings.description: The PDF presents the agenda as a dotted-leader list rather than a formal table. The HTML converts this into a structured table with explicit headers. While the data content is preserved accurately, the format is changed.
findings.evidence: PDF uses dotted leaders (e.g., 'Introductions, opening remarks ........ Tom Fransen, DWR') while HTML uses a 3-column table structure.

findings.description: The document has nested <main> elements. 'Stakeholder Meeting #2' is rendered as h2, which is reasonable, but the heading hierarchy could be seen as slightly off since in the PDF both lines form the title together.
findings.evidence: PDF title block: 'New and Watauga Hydrologic Model / Stakeholder Meeting #2' — HTML splits into h1 and h2.
